Name: spectral_precision_routing
Title: Spectral Precision Routing: Training-Free Adaptive Precision Allocation via Activation Geometry
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that the local covariance condition number and spectral decay rate of hidden states serve as direct proxies for quantization sensitivity, enabling training-free adaptive precision allocation in machine learning models.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Spectral Latent Steering (SLS), which demonstrate the potential of spectral methods for efficient inference. However, our approach differs in its focus on training-free adaptive precision allocation and its use of activation geometry for quantization sensitivity estimation.
Abstract: We propose Spectral Precision Routing, a training-free framework that dynamically allocates numerical precision based on real-time activation geometry. Our approach computes local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates of hidden states, mapping them to a precision-switching policy that enforces strict latency budgets. We theoretically ground the routing mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral conditioning to generalization stability under quantization noise. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed framework on WILDS benchmarks using single-GPU setups
Compare the performance of our approach with static and heuristic mixed-precision baselines
Investigate the effect of different precision-switching policies on throughput-accuracy tradeoffs
Analyze the robustness of our approach to distributional shifts and noisy inputs
Risk Factors and Limitations: The accuracy of the proposed framework may be sensitive to the choice of spectral methods and hyperparameters
The computational overhead of computing local covariance condition numbers and spectral decay rates may be non-negligible
The approach may not be effective for models with complex or non-linear activation functions
